---
title: "Causal Inference and Counterfactuals"
description: "Study note on Causal Inference and Counterfactuals in Impact Evaluation in Practice."
book_id: "impact-evaluation-in-practice"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Major section"
source_pages: "77-78"
citation_key: "gertler_martinez_premand_rawlings_vermeersch_2016_impact_evaluation_in_practice"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:causal-inference:c0001"
source_status: "needs_review"
---
## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2016</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Source pages 77-78</span><span>Major section</span></div>
This note summarises the MCP research-library section "Causal Inference and Counterfactuals" from Paul J. Gertler, Sebastian Martinez, Patrick Premand, Laura B. Rawlings, and Christel M. J. Vermeersch's *Impact Evaluation in Practice* [@gertler_martinez_premand_rawlings_vermeersch_2016_impact_evaluation_in_practice]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked retrieval records, not a substitute for the source text.

## Core Argument

The section explains why before-and-after observation alone is not sufficient for causal inference.

## Study Summary

The section explains why before-and-after observation alone is not sufficient for causal inference.

Impact evaluation requires an estimate of what would have happened to participants without the program.

The central design problem is therefore to construct a credible counterfactual.

## Key Concepts

- Causal inference
- Counterfactual
- Attribution
- Outcome comparison

## Source Location

- Section path: Part 2: How to Evaluate > Chapter 3: Causal Inference and Counterfactuals > Causal Inference
- Evidence records: `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:causal-inference:c0001`

## Practical Implications

- Identify the counterfactual strategy before collecting outcome data.
- Avoid interpreting observed change as impact without a comparison logic.
- Explain the assumptions that make the comparison credible.

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Concepts: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Map: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What is the main evaluation problem that Causal Inference and Counterfactuals helps diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before using this guidance in a live evaluation?
3. How would this change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, or reporting?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## References
